title: Booleans & Chars 🔠
description: True, False, and every emoji under the sun.

slides.title: Booleans ⚖️
slides.content:
A boolean type in Rust has two possible values: `true` and `false`.

Booleans are exactly one byte in size. They are defined using the `bool` type.

slides.title: Declaring Booleans 🟢🔴
slides.content:
```rust
let is_active = true;
let is_greater: bool = false; // with explicit type annotation
```

Booleans are heavily used in control flow (like if-statements) which we will see in the next chapter.

quizData.question: What are the two possible values for a boolean?
quizData.explanation: Booleans strictly evaluate to `true` or `false` in Rust.

slides.title: The Char Type 🅰️
slides.content:
Rust’s `char` type is the language’s most primitive alphabetic type.

```rust
let letter = 'z'; // Note the SINGLE quotes
let emoji = '🦀'; 
```

Notice how characters use single quotes `''`, while strings use double quotes `""`.

slides.title: Unicode Power 🌎
slides.content:
Rust's `char` type represents a **Unicode Scalar Value**.

This means a `char` isn't just an ASCII letter; it can be an accented character, a Chinese/Japanese/Korean glyph, or an emoji! (Takes 4 bytes of memory).

quizData.question: How do you distinguish a `char` from a String when declaring it?

options.text: String uses single quotes ('a'), char uses double quotes ("a")
char uses single quotes ('a'), String uses double quotes ("a")

quizData.explanation: In Rust syntax, individual scalar characters must be wrapped in single quotes, while lists of characters (Strings) must be double quotes.
